assembly_instructions: Full launch-configuration Apollo CSM stack. From bottom up: bell-shaped Service Propulsion System nozzle, cylindrical Service Module carrying four 90°-spaced RCS thruster quads, a deployed S-band high-gain antenna mast + dish cluster, a Scientific Instrument Module (SIM) bay on the opposite side, four radiator panels wrapping the SM between the RCS quads, and the crew umbilical fairing at the SM/CM junction. The conical Command Module is approximated as four stacked truncated cylinders (aft heat shield, lower, mid, forward compartment) and carries a side hatch and an apex docking probe. A Launch Escape System tower rises from the CM apex, topped by the solid escape rocket, a jettison motor, and a Q-ball nose cap. Real-scale 0.5× — ≈5 m tall, 1.95 m diameter SM.

info.japanese_name: アポロ司令船・機械船 (CSM)
info.english_name: NASA Apollo Command and Service Module (CSM)
info.summary: Crewed spacecraft used on all Apollo lunar missions. A conical 3-seat Command Module rode home on the heat shield; a cylindrical Service Module carried propulsion, power, and life support.
info.description: The Apollo Command and Service Module (CSM) was the crew-carrying spacecraft of NASA's Apollo program. It consisted of two mated sections: the Command Module (CM) — a pressurized conical capsule 3.48 m tall with a 3.91 m-diameter ablative heat shield at its base, housing three astronauts and returning them to Earth; and the Service Module (SM) — a 4.52 m-long, 3.91 m-diameter cylindrical stage carrying the Service Propulsion System (SPS) main engine with a 2.50 m-diameter bell nozzle, four Reaction Control System (RCS) thruster quads at 90° spacing, the deployable S-band high-gain antenna (four 0.79 m dishes + a central square), radiator panels, fuel cells, cryogenic propellant, and on J-missions a Scientific Instrument Module (SIM) bay recessed into one sector. A Launch Escape System tower mounted above the CM during ascent could pull the capsule clear of a failing booster; once safe staging was achieved it was jettisoned, exposing the docking probe used to mate with the Lunar Module. NASA engineering documentation for the CSM is in the public domain.
info.contractor: North American Aviation / Rockwell (CM); North American Aviation (SM)
info.contract_date: Development contract 1961; flew Apollo 7 (Oct 1968) through Apollo-Soyuz (Jul 1975)
info.status: Retired; flown on 15 crewed Apollo/Skylab/ASTP missions

facts.label: HGA
facts.value: Four 0.79 m dishes + central 0.28 m square reflector, deployable

facts.label: RCS
facts.value: 4 × quad clusters at 90° around the SM (16 thrusters total)

facts.label: License
facts.value: NASA imagery / engineering docs — public domain

sources.title: Wikipedia — Apollo command and service module

parts.name: SPS Nozzle Exit Ring
parts.role: Flared exit lip of the Service Propulsion System bell nozzle (2.50 m exit diameter scaled 0.5×).

parts.name: SPS Nozzle Bell
parts.role: Bell-profile nozzle of the SPS main engine. Lunar-orbit-insertion and trans-Earth-injection burns fire through here.

parts.name: Service Module (SM)
parts.role: Cylindrical service stage. Carries the SPS engine, four RCS quads, fuel cells, propellant, radiators, HGA, and (on J-missions) the SIM bay.

parts.name: RCS Quad (+X)
parts.role: Four-thruster Reaction Control System cluster on the +X face of the SM.

parts.name: RCS Quad (−X)
parts.role: Four-thruster RCS cluster on the −X face of the SM.

parts.name: RCS Quad (+Z)
parts.role: Four-thruster RCS cluster on the +Z face of the SM.

parts.name: RCS Quad (−Z)
parts.role: Four-thruster RCS cluster on the −Z face of the SM.

parts.name: SM Radiator Panel (I)
parts.role: One of the four flat radiator panels wrapping the SM between the RCS quads — rejects waste heat from the environmental control system and fuel cells.

parts.name: SM Radiator Panel (II)
parts.role: Second radiator panel, 90° around from the first.

parts.name: SIM Bay (Scientific Instrument Module)
parts.role: Recessed science bay on the −X sector of the SM, added for the J-missions (Apollo 15–17). Carried mapping and panoramic cameras and a sub-satellite.

parts.name: HGA Deployable Mast
parts.role: Deployable boom that swings the S-band high-gain antenna out clear of the SM for Earth-pointing.

parts.name: HGA 4-Dish Cluster
parts.role: Four 0.79 m parabolic dishes + central 0.28 m square reflector. S-band command and telemetry link with Earth. Approximated as a single flat disk.

parts.name: Crew Umbilical Fairing
parts.role: Fairing at the SM/CM interface housing the umbilical wiring between the two modules.

parts.name: CM Aft Heat Shield
parts.role: Ablative aft heat shield at the base of the Command Module — protects the crew during re-entry at ≈11 km/s.

parts.name: CM Lower Cone
parts.role: Lower segment of the conical Command Module pressure shell (approximated as a truncated cylinder).

parts.name: CM Mid Cone
parts.role: Middle segment of the conical CM — carries the crew couches and main control panel.

parts.name: CM Forward Compartment
parts.role: Forward compartment — houses the drogue parachutes, tunnel, and hatch to the docking probe.

parts.name: CM Side Hatch
parts.role: Unified side hatch on the +X face of the CM — primary crew ingress/egress.

parts.name: Docking Probe
parts.role: Central probe at the CM apex — engages the Lunar Module's drogue for docking on the trans-lunar coast.

parts.name: Launch Escape Tower
parts.role: Four-bar truss between the CM apex and the LES solid rocket (approximated as a single thin cylinder). Jettisoned ≈3.5 minutes into flight if the mission is nominal.

parts.name: LES Solid Escape Rocket
parts.role: Solid-propellant rocket — fires to pull the CM clear of a failing booster.

parts.name: LES Jettison Motor
parts.role: Small solid motor at the top of the LES — pulls the spent escape rocket away once safe staging is reached.

parts.name: LES Q-Ball Nose Cap
parts.role: Aerodynamic Q-ball nose cap — senses dynamic pressure and angle-of-attack during early ascent.
